content: If $ y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-x}{1+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right) $, then $ y^{\prime}(1) $ is equal to
explanation:
Given the function $ y = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-x}{1+x^{\frac{3}{2}}}\right) $.

We can rewrite this expression using an identity for inverse tangent:

$ y = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}-x}{1+\sqrt{x} \cdot x}\right) $

This can be expressed as:

$ y = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{x}) - \tan^{-1}(x) $

This transformation uses the identity:

$ \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a-b}{1+ab}\right) = \tan^{-1}(a) - \tan^{-1}(b) $

To find the derivative $ y' $ with respect to $ x $, we use the differentiation of inverse tangent functions:

$ y' = \frac{d}{dx}[\tan^{-1}(\sqrt{x}) - \tan^{-1}(x)] $

The derivative is calculated as follows:

$ y' = \frac{1}{1+(\sqrt{x})^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} - \frac{1}{1+x^2} $

Now, evaluate $ y'(1) $:

$ y'(1) = \frac{1}{1+1} \cdot \frac{1}{2\cdot1} - \frac{1}{1+1} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = -\frac{1}{4} $

Thus, the value of $ y'(1) $ is $ -\frac{1}{4} $.

Given, $$\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})-\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=x$$ ... (i)

We know that $$\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos } \alpha)+\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=\frac{\pi}{2}$$ .... (ii)

On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \cot ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=\frac{\pi}{2}+x \\ & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\cos } \alpha=\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)=\frac{\cot \frac{x}{2}-1}{1+\cot \frac{x}{2}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\cos \alpha}=\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}$$

On squaring both sides, we get

$$\cos \alpha=\frac{\cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}-2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}{\cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}+\sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}+2 \sin \frac{x}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1-\sin x}{1+\sin x} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}{1+\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}=\frac{1-\sin x}{1+\sin x} \end{aligned}$$

On applying componendo and dividendo rule, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}-1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}{1-\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}+1+\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}=\frac{1-\sin x-1-\sin x}{1-\sin x+1+\sin x} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}=\sin x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \sin x=\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2} \end{aligned}$$
